preamble: The transformation from a voiced uvular plosive /ɢ/ to a voiceless uvular fricative /χ/ is a phonetic shift characterized by lenition, specifically involving both spirantization (the change from a plosive to a fricative) and devoicing (the loss of vocal cord vibration). This process typically occurs in specific phonological environments, such as within consonant clusters.

examples.note: In word-medial consonant clusters, the voiced uvular plosive /ɢ/ can undergo spirantization and devoicing to become a voiceless uvular fricative [χ], as seen in words like /motɢan/ becoming [moˈtχan] 'solid'.

sources: Makarov, Y. (2023). Uvular stop lenition and Shughni. Conference: Minor languages in general linguistics.
Ghaffari, M., & Ghasemi, M. (2018). ACOUSTICS OF PERSIAN UVULAR LENITION IN CONSONANT CLUSTERS*. Canadian Linguistic Association.
